problem: Ch16 uses 'something' at 1 per 440 words, well above the 1-per-800 threshold. In Joel's deduction chapter, the hedge weakens the logical precision that defines his voice.
rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch16 to no more than 2 instances. Replace with specific nouns or observations.

acceptance_test: Ch5 contains no more than 3 instances of 'something.'
evidence: 'Something' appears 108 times manuscript-wide (1 per 604 words). Ch5 contains 8 instances in 3,828 words (1 per 478 words).
problem: Ch5 uses 'something' at 1 per 478 words in Mallick's first POV chapter. Mallick is observationally precise; the hedge undercuts her voice.
rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch5 to no more than 3 instances. Mallick should name what she notices rather than defer to 'something.'

acceptance_test: Ch16 does not quote more than 2-3 sentences of the fire document. Joel's reading is conveyed through reaction and selective excerpt rather than full reproduction.
evidence: Ch8/2044 and Ch16/4240 both reproduce Martin's fire document opening: 'I'm writing this down because somebody should, and because I don't trust myself to remember it right if I don't write it now while it's still clear.' The opening sentence is verbatim across chapters because it is the same document. However, the document content then diverges significantly (see consistency finding C01).
problem: Martin's fire document is reproduced at length in both Ch8 (lines 2042-2066, ~600 words) and Ch16 (lines 4238-4256, ~500 words). Even accounting for it being the same document, the second full reproduction is a near-verbatim passage that the reader has already absorbed. Ch8 establishes the document; Ch16 should not re-quote it in full.
rewrite_direction: In Ch16, do not reproduce the full document text. Joel can read silently; the chapter can excerpt only the details NEW to Joel (or that hit differently from his perspective) and summarize the rest in a sentence. The opening line need not be repeated.

acceptance_test: The fire document text in Ch8 and Ch16 matches on: date written, date of fire, structure involved, cause of fire, location of Toomey, and fire ruling.
evidence: Ch8/2042 dates the document 'August 17, 2002' and describes the fire on 'the night of August 11, 2002.' Ch16/4238 dates the same document 'August 14, 2002' with no specific fire-night date. Ch8: kerosene heater knocked over in cabin kitchen, Toomey in back bedroom, fire ruled 'accidental/faulty heater.' Ch16: gasoline can near space heater in 'the shed,' Toomey in the shed, fire ruled 'electrical.' Ch8: Martin drove back alone at 3 AM to reposition heater. Ch16: Martin 'didn't tell the truth in any useful way' but no return trip described.
problem: Martin's fire document is the same physical document read by Cliff in Ch8 and Joel in Ch16, but the two chapters present it with different dates, different fire causes, different structures (cabin kitchen vs. shed), a different location for the victim (back bedroom vs. shed), and a different fire ruling (accidental/faulty heater vs. electrical). These cannot be perspective differences — the document is one artifact.
rewrite_direction: Reconcile the fire document to a single consistent version. Choose one date, one cause, one structure, one fire ruling, and one version of Martin's role. Propagate the chosen version to both Ch8 and Ch16. The Ch12 Tommy-memory version may intentionally diverge (unreliable memory), but the written document must be internally consistent.

acceptance_test: The cash amount in the safe is consistent across Ch8 and Ch11, or an intervening removal is explicitly established.
evidence: Ch8/2008: 'Cliff took the cash first and counted without removing the clip. Twenties and fifties. Roughly two thousand. He put it back.' Ch11/2739: 'A smaller envelope with cash. Paul counted it automatically... Eight hundred and sixty dollars in twenties and fifties.'
problem: The cash in Martin's safe is 'roughly two thousand' when Cliff counts it (Ch8/2008, Sunday morning) but 'eight hundred and sixty dollars' when Paul counts it (Ch11/2739, Monday). Cliff explicitly puts the cash back. No character is described removing cash between these chapters.
rewrite_direction: Reconcile the cash amount. Either make both counts match (e.g., both ~$860 or both ~$2,000), or add a brief note that someone took cash from the safe between Sunday and Monday if that is the intended plot.

acceptance_test: Cliff's car is the same make in Ch1 and Ch8.
evidence: Ch1/7: 'Nate parked behind Cliff's Audi. The Audi was new, or new-looking.' Ch8/2004: 'Cliff's Lexus. The Lexus was leased. Fourteen hundred a month.'
problem: Cliff's vehicle is an Audi in Ch1/7 and a Lexus in Ch8/2004. These are different makes. Only one car was driven to the lake house.
rewrite_direction: Pick one make and correct the other. Given Cliff's financial stress narrative in Ch8 (leased, fourteen hundred a month), the Lexus detail may be more thematically functional. If so, change Ch1/7 'Audi' to 'Lexus.'

acceptance_test: The number of photos on Cliff's phone matches the number taken in Ch8.
evidence: Ch8/2082: 'Cliff photographed each page, front and back, four sheets, eight images.' Ch8/2116: 'what those eight photos were worth.' Ch18/4668: 'Cliff Haywood's phone with fourteen photos of that document.'
problem: Cliff takes 8 photos (4 sheets x 2 sides) in Ch8, but Ch18 says his phone contained 14 photos of the document. 8 and 14 do not match.
rewrite_direction: Change Ch18/4668 from 'fourteen photos' to 'eight photos' to match the established count in Ch8.

acceptance_test: The draft board column count in Ch18 matches the number of league members (seven).
evidence: Ch16/4382: 'seven columns of names in different colors from Thursday night' (correct for a 7-man league). Ch18/4878: 'Twelve columns, eighteen rounds, names written in his small steady hand.'
problem: The draft board has 7 columns in Ch16 (correct — one per league member) but 12 columns in Ch18. A seven-man league would have seven columns.
rewrite_direction: Change Ch18/4878 from 'Twelve columns' to 'Seven columns.'

acceptance_test: The safe contents in Ch8 and Ch11 are reconciled: same number of folders, same envelope size, same cash container, and the will's presence or absence in Ch8 is explained.
evidence: Ch8/2008: 'Inside were three manila folders, a small law-office envelope, one legal-size manila envelope, and a stack of bills clipped together.' Ch11/2739: 'a manila folder of financial documents... A smaller envelope with cash... A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized... And beneath everything, in a clear plastic sleeve, a document.'
problem: The safe contents inventory differs between Ch8 and Ch11. Three manila folders become one. The 'legal-size' envelope becomes 'letter-sized.' The 'law-office envelope' disappears. Martin's will (in a clear plastic sleeve) appears in Ch11 but was not mentioned in Ch8, despite Cliff examining the safe contents. The 'stack of bills clipped together' becomes 'a smaller envelope with cash.'
rewrite_direction: Reconcile the safe contents across Ch8 and Ch11. Either (a) make both inventories match exactly, or (b) if Cliff's inventory is deliberately incomplete (he was focused on the debt), note explicitly in Ch8 that he didn't examine everything. The will's absence from Ch8 is the most critical gap — Cliff should either notice and dismiss it, or the will should not be visible on casual inspection.

acceptance_test: The truck color is consistent, or the discrepancy is acknowledged as memory uncertainty.
evidence: Ch8/2048 (Martin's document): 'His blue Ford pickup was still in the drive Friday night.' Ch12/2986 (Tommy's memory): 'Green Ford pickup. Dull paint. Mud on the wheel wells.'
problem: Ray Toomey's truck is blue in Martin's written account (Ch8) and green in Tommy's memory (Ch12). The written account was composed days after the event and should be more reliable than a 22-year-old memory. If this is intentional unreliable memory, it is not signaled to the reader.
rewrite_direction: Either match the truck color across both accounts, or have Tommy's narration acknowledge uncertainty about the detail ('green, maybe blue, the color was already fading in his memory').

acceptance_test: The RT envelope has a consistent size and seal state across Ch8 and Ch11.
evidence: Ch8/2026: describes the RT envelope as 'the legal-size envelope' with 'a metal clasp that had been bent open and closed often enough to loosen it.' Ch11/2739: 'A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized, with the letters RT written on it.' Ch8 says the clasp was loose from repeated opening; Ch11 says the envelope is 'sealed' with 'flap unbroken' (line 2773).
problem: The RT envelope is 'legal-size' with a loose, oft-opened clasp in Ch8, but 'letter-sized' and 'sealed' with 'flap unbroken' in Ch11. The physical state of the envelope contradicts between chapters — it cannot be both repeatedly opened and sealed/unbroken.
rewrite_direction: Decide the canonical state: was the envelope previously opened (Ch8's loose clasp) or sealed (Ch11's unbroken flap)? If Cliff opened it in Ch8, Paul should find it re-closed but not sealed. Reconcile size to one designation.

acceptance_test: The will appears in Cliff's safe inventory in Ch8, even if he does not read it in detail.
evidence: Ch8/2008-2014: Cliff inventories the safe and finds three folders (HOUSE, INVESTMENTS, PERSONAL), the RT envelope, a law-office envelope, and cash. He opens and reads the RT envelope. Ch11/2739: Paul finds 'a manila folder of financial documents... A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized... a document [the will] in a clear plastic sleeve.'
problem: Cliff examined the safe contents in detail in Ch8 but did not see or mention Martin's will. Paul finds the will in Ch11 in a 'clear plastic sleeve' beneath everything. If the will was in the safe when Cliff opened it Sunday morning, Cliff either saw it and ignored it (implausible given his financial motive) or it was not there yet (impossible — no one else put it in). The most likely explanation is that the will was omitted from Ch8 in error.
rewrite_direction: Add the will to Cliff's inventory in Ch8. Cliff can notice it, register that it is Martin's will, and choose not to read it carefully because he is focused on the debt documentation and the RT envelope. This preserves the plot while closing the gap.

acceptance_test: Martin's described role in the fire aftermath is the same in both Ch8 and Ch16 versions of the document.
evidence: Ch8/2058-2060 (Martin's document): 'Martin had driven back at three in the morning. Alone. Work gloves from the trunk. He repositioned the heater to make it look like it failed where it stood. He adjusted what he could of the spill pattern. The fire investigation ruled it accidental.' Ch16/4248 (Martin's document): 'The fire department ruled it electrical. Tommy told them what he told me. I didn't contradict him.'
problem: In the Ch8 version of Martin's document, Martin returns alone to the scene at 3 AM and physically tampers with evidence (repositions the heater, adjusts the spill). In the Ch16 version, Martin merely stays silent ('I didn't contradict him'). This changes Martin's culpability from active evidence tampering to passive omission — a significant difference for the moral weight of the story.
rewrite_direction: This is part of the same fire-document reconciliation as C01. Choose one version of Martin's role (active tampering or passive silence) and apply it consistently across Ch8 and Ch16.

acceptance_test: The day the fire occurs is consistent between the two renderings of Martin's document. Tommy's memory in Ch12 either matches or is flagged as uncertain.
evidence: Ch8/2048: Tommy had been 'drinking since they'd left Minneapolis.' Ch16/4242: 'Tommy was drinking heavy on Saturday, worse than usual, and usual was already a lot. Around 9 PM he went outside to smoke.' Ch12/2972: 'Friday night. Bonfire. Too much lighter fluid.'
problem: The fire document in Ch8 says they arrived and the fire happened the same night (Tommy drinking 'since they'd left Minneapolis'). The Ch16 version says they'd been there 'since Friday' and the fire happened Saturday night. Ch12 Tommy's memory says 'Friday night.' The day of the fire is inconsistent across all three versions.
rewrite_direction: Reconcile as part of the fire-document reconciliation (C01). The document should specify one consistent day. Tommy's memory in Ch12 may diverge intentionally but should not contradict the document on basic timeline.
